title: Narrow Nightstand with Drawer
summary: A narrow, deep plywood nightstand with a face frame, one full-width drawer, and a cove-trimmed 1x8 top.
description:
Most nightstand plans are cubes; this one is deliberately narrow (17" wide) and deep, which suits a small bedroom footprint without giving up drawer volume — the depth carries the storage that the width gives up.

The carcass is plywood, faced with a solid-wood face frame, which is what lets the drawer front sit flush with a consistent reveal instead of showing raw plywood edges around the opening. The tabletop's edges are dressed in **cove moulding**, a cheap way to turn a flat-sawn board edge into a profiled one without a router table.

A matching dresser design exists from the same source if you want the pieces to read as a set — not included here, since this catalog covers one plan per entry.

tools.note: Breaks down the plywood and rips the face-frame stock.
Attaches the back panel and trim.

cutList.note: Cut to fit the assembled carcass.

steps.title: Cut the carcass and solid-wood pieces
steps.body: Cut the plywood carcass sides, bottom shelf, and back panel, along with the face frame, base trim, and tabletop boards, per the cut list.

steps.title: Build the basic box
steps.body: Join the bottom shelf between the two side panels with pocket screws and glue to form the carcass.

steps.title: Square it up with the back
steps.body: Add the 1x3 back support with pocket holes, then nail on the plywood back panel — this squares the carcass and locks it in place.

steps.title: Attach the face frame and base trim
steps.body: Build the 1x2 face frame to match the carcass width and attach it to the front. Add the base trim around the bottom and the drawer-slide cleats inside the carcass.

steps.title: Build and hang the drawer
steps.body: Build the drawer box from 1x4 stock with a plywood bottom, mount it on the slides so it sits inset behind the face frame, then attach the 1x8 drawer face with a consistent reveal gap all around.

steps.title: Build the top and finish
steps.body: Glue and screw the tabletop boards together with a consistent overhang on the front and sides, then dress the exposed edges with cove moulding. Fill nail holes, sand with the grain, then paint or stain.
